attributes.stem:
A four-year-old boy presents to the Emergency Department with his mother after experiencing a "strange turn" while playing with his friends in the kindergarten playground. The boy was running around the corner at top speed when he tripped over and landed on his front, scraping his knee on the concrete. He looked like he was about to burst into tears but suddenly went limp and his body began to twitch. His face also became very pale. This episode lasted for about 15 seconds before he returned to normal colour and began crying from his injury.

On presentation to the hospital, he appeared back to his normal self. He was born at term with no significant antenatal, birth or postnatal complications. He has no significant medical history, takes no regular medications and is up to date with immunisations.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely cause of this episode?
attributes.explanation:
This is the typical picture of a breath holding spell. Such an occurrence may be a distressing event to observe in paediatric patients, but it is a benign condition with no long-term or detrimental health effects. Breath holding spells are a common phenomenon for children between the ages of six months to six years and can occur multiple times a day. They occur most often as a result of the child sustaining a fright, having an accident, becoming frustrated or upset. The exact mechanism by which breath holding spells occur is not fully understood. However, current literature suggests there may be a link to iron deficiency anaemia. There are two types of breath holding spells - pallid breath holding and cyanotic breath holding.

Pallid breath holding (pale spells) are less common but occur more often in association with pain or being startled. In this type of spell, the child's face will often turn pale due to bradycardia. This is the type of spell occurring in the above scenario.

Cyanotic breath-holding (blue spells) are the more common form of breath holding spells that often occurs in response to frustration, anger or being reprimanded. In this type of spell, the child might forcefully cry out, then hold their breath and turn blue around the lips as a result of the period of apnoea causing cyanosis.

Other features of breath holding spells include that they are brief (usually between 10-30 seconds) and may be associated with fainting (going floppy) and a brief period of twitching in the limbs. Children may also become incontinent in these episodes which makes an epileptic seizure high on the differential list.

The difference between the two differentials is that a breath holding spell is often precipitated by an inciting event such as pain, whereas this may not always be the case in epileptic seizures. Similarly, seizures that are commonly generalised, tonic-clonic in nature may last longer than 30 seconds, be associated with an epileptic cry, have more exaggerated muscle tone followed by jerking and have a period of confusion in the post-ictal period.

Vasovagal syncope is another condition that can cause sudden loss of consciousness in children. However, it typically occurs in older children and adolescents, and is often associated with prolonged standing, dehydration, or emotional stress. The rapid recovery and young age of the patient make this less likely in this case.

Reflex anoxic seizure, also known as pallid syncope, can present similarly to a breath holding spell. It is triggered by a sudden fright or pain, leading to brief asystole and loss of consciousness. While children can appear as though they were about to cry with reflex anoxic seizures, these episodes typically involve an immediate loss of consciousness without a period of breath holding. In contrast, breath holding spells usually involve a brief period where the child appears to be holding their breath before losing consciousness. Additionally, breath holding spells are more common than reflex anoxic seizures.

Cardiac arrhythmia is an important consideration in any child with syncope, especially if associated with exercise. However, the clear precipitating event (falling and scraping his knee) and the rapid, complete recovery make this less likely in this case. If there were recurrent episodes or a family history of sudden cardiac death, further cardiac evaluation would be warranted.
learningPoints: Breath holding spells are distressing episodes to observe in paediatric patients, however they are a benign phenomenon.
Breath holding spells can be differentiated from seizures by their brief duration, typical precipitating events, and rapid return to normal.
Breath holding spells and reflex anoxic seizures can present similarly, but breath holding spells are more common and typically involve a period of breath holding before loss of consciousness.
references: Breningstall GN. Breath-holding spells. Pediatr Neurol. 1996 Feb;14(2):91-7. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8703234/

Leung AK, Leung AA, Wong AH, Hon KL. Breath-holding spells in pediatrics: a narrative review of the current evidence. Current pediatric reviews. 2019 Feb 1;15(1):22-9. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30421679/
Hood BM, Harbord MG. Paediatric narcolepsy: complexities of diagnosis. Journal of paediatrics and child health. 2002 Dec;38(6):618-21. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12410881/
